intro: Camunda and Cerebras bill differently, which matters more than feature parity here — Camunda sells a subscription from $100 with a free tier, while Cerebras offers a free tier, paid from $50/month. Camunda is BPMN workflow and decision automation engine for orchestrating APIs and services. Cerebras is hosted inference on Cerebras hardware for very fast LLM token throughput. Pick Camunda when the job is api gateway and rate limiting and you accept low lock-in; pick Cerebras when it is api gateway and rate limiting and you accept low lock-in. The data we have shows Camunda at some public pricing / roadmap detail with a polished developer experience, and Cerebras at mostly transparent pricing and docs with solid developer experience. The honest trade-off: neither is universal — Camunda is a poor fit for internal, simple projects, and Cerebras is a poor fit for internal, simple projects. Match the pricing model and lock-in level to how your team actually works, not the feature list.
quick_take: Camunda is for BPMN workflow and decision automation engine; Cerebras is for hosted inference on Cerebras hardware; decide based on pricing model fit.
verdict_a: Choose Camunda if a subscription at $100 and you want an entry-level free tier fits the budget and the work maps to BPMN workflow and decision automation engine for orchestrating APIs and services.
verdict_b: Choose Cerebras if you need public pricing and docs before committing and the work lines up with hosted inference on Cerebras hardware for very fast LLM token throughput.

faq.a: Camunda starts at $100 on a subscription model; Cerebras starts at $50/month on a freemium model. That makes the sticker comparison depend on usage — a low entry price can get expensive once volume ramps, so price at your actual workload.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Camunda to Cerebras?
faq.a: Migration in either direction is relatively cheap — both Camunda and Cerebras are rated low lock-in, so your configuration and data should port without a rewrite. The realistic cost is team re-training and pipeline QA, not the tools themselves.

Camunda scores higher on developer experience in our data (5/5 vs 4/5). That reflects public feedback on docs, onboarding, and day-to-day ergonomics. Still, run a spike with your own code — DX ratings don't capture every edge.

faq.q: Is Cerebras a good alternative to Camunda?
faq.a: Yes — Cerebras is a reasonable alternative to Camunda for api gateway and rate limiting. The practical differences are subscription-vs-freemium billing and low-vs-low lock-in. If those fit your constraints better, treat Cerebras as a credible swap.
